Author: Roberto Infante
Title: Mastering Cursor IDE: 10 Best Practices (Building a Daily Task Manager App)
Text:
Mastering Cursor IDE: 10 Best Practices (Building a Daily Task Manager App)
Cursor is an AI-powered code editor that deeply integrates large language models into your development workflow. In plain terms, it’s like an always-on pair programmer inside your IDE — one that understands your project and actively assists in writing, editing, and debugging code. Transitioning to Cursor (from a traditional editor or a simple ChatGPT plugin) can be a major productivity boost because it blurs the line between coding and conversing with an expert assistant. Instead of just autocompleting code, Cursor can comprehend your entire codebase, follow high-level instructions, and even execute tasks autonomously. This means you can delegate routine coding chores or complex refactors to an AI agent while you steer the high-level design. The result isn’t just writing code faster; it’s coding smarter — focusing your effort on ideas and architecture while the AI handles the boilerplate and busywork.
In this article, we’ll walk through ten best practices for getting the most out of Cursor, structured around building a Daily Task Manager app (a simple to-do list web application). We’ll start with planning (using Cursor to generate a Product Requirements Document) and end with advanced tips (like leveraging MCP servers for extra muscle). Whether you’re a newcomer to Cursor or looking to level up your AI-assisted development, these tips will help you use Cursor strategically — because used naïvely (as a glorified autocomplete), it might disappoint, but used wisely, Cursor becomes a force multiplier for productivity.
Overview: The 10 Best Practices
- Generate a PRD File — Begin with a clear Product Requirements Document. Use Cursor’s AI to draft this blueprint so you have a “North Star” for development.
- Set Project Rules — Define custom Cursor rules for your tech stack (e.g. FastAPI backend, Streamlit frontend). These guide the AI’s coding style and architecture decisions.
- Choose the Right Agent Mode — Understand when to use Cursor’s AGENT mode versus ASK mode. Agent mode can autonomously execute tasks, while Ask mode is for Q&A and exploration.
- Select the Best Model — Pick an appropriate AI model for the job. High-quality models (Claude-4 Sonnet, OpenAI’s latest “o3”, or Google’s Gemini 2.5 Pro) produce better code, whereas “Max” models like Claude-4 Opus offer huge context windows for large projects. Have GPT-4.1 (or the older GPT-4o) as fallbacks for simpler completions.
- Use
@
References – Provide context by referencing files, web pages, code, or terminal outputs directly in prompts. For example,@File
to inject a file’s content,@Web
to pull in web info,@Code
to refer to code snippets, and@Terminal
to include console logs. - Write Detailed Prompts — Be explicit about what you want. Clearly specify requirements, filenames, and even line numbers if needed. Precise, detailed prompts yield more accurate results.
- Request Logging, Unit Tests, and Documentation — Don’t just ask for feature code. Have Cursor include logging for debug visibility, generate unit tests to catch issues, and produce docstrings or README updates. It’s worth the extra effort for long-term quality.
- Improve Prompts Iteratively — Treat each AI generation as a draft. Review Cursor’s output, refine your instructions, and run again. Each iteration will improve the code as you incorporate learnings.
- Exclude Unnecessary Files from Indexing — Speed up Cursor and reduce confusion by ignoring large or irrelevant files. Use
.cursorignore
/.cursorindexignore
to prevent indexing of dependencies, binaries, or docs you don’t need in context. - Utilize MCP Servers (if available) — For advanced users, consider hooking Cursor up to an MCP (Model Context Protocol) server. These provide extended knowledge or context (e.g. framework documentation) on demand. It’s an optional power-up that can supercharge Cursor on big projects.
With the overview in mind, let’s dive into each best practice in detail, applying them to our Daily Task Manager app scenario.
1. Generate a PRD File
Every successful project starts with a plan. In software, that plan is often a Product Requirements Document (PRD) — a blueprint that defines the app’s purpose, features, target users, tech stack, and success criteria. In our case, the Daily Task Manager app’s PRD would outline what “tasks” are (title, description, status), how users create or complete them, the UI design (columns for To-Do, In Progress, Done), and so on. Cursor can help you draft this document before you write any code.
Why do this first? A PRD serves as your “North Star” to guide development. It ensures both you and the AI agent are aligned on the end goal. In Cursor, you can actually have the AI generate a PRD from scratch or flesh out your outline. This not only saves time but also uncovers details you might miss. As one Cursor power-user notes, spending time up front on a good PRD (with tech stack, constraints, clear goals, and detailed stories) leads to much better outcomesforum.cursor.com — the more granular your plan, the more effectively the AI will implement it.
Sample Prompt (to Cursor’s agent): “You are a software product manager. Help me create a Product Requirements Document for a Daily Task Manager Web App. Include: project purpose, user stories (adding tasks, marking tasks done, editing tasks, etc.), key features (drag-and-drop task board with columns for statuses, data persistence in a database, priority labels, search/filter), non-functional requirements (responsive design, performance), and the tech stack we plan to use (e.g. Python FastAPI for backend API, Streamlit for frontend). Organize the PRD with clear sections and bullet points.”
When you feed such a prompt to Cursor, the AGENT will likely produce a well-structured PRD in Markdown — perhaps saved as an instructions.md
or PRD.md
file. Review this output and edit any inaccuracies (for example, ensure it mentions all features you want). This PRD can then be referenced during coding (we’ll see how to use it with @File
in prompts later). It essentially becomes the project’s single source of truth that the AI can refer back to when generating code, so you don’t have to repeat requirements in every prompt.
2. Set Project Rules
Once the requirements are set, it’s time to set some ground rules — literally. Cursor Rules are one of the most powerful (and underrated) features of Cursor IDE. They allow you to define custom guidelines that shape the AI’s code generation behavior for your specific project. Think of them as a project-specific coding style guide and SOP for the AI. By creating rule files, you can tell Cursor “We’re using FastAPI for the backend, so prefer async endpoints and Pydantic models” or “Our frontend is Streamlit, so use Streamlit APIs for UI, not generic HTML.” The AI will then follow these instructions consistently, which leads to more relevant and standardized code output.
Cursor rules can encode anything from architectural patterns to library choices to code style. For example, you might enforce a rule that “All database access must go through repository classes” or “Use logging for every API call”. With rules in place, the AI’s suggestions align with your project’s needs and conventions, rather than generic boilerplate. This yields more accurate code and less rework for you.
How do you define rules? In Cursor, you can create a .cursor/rules/
directory in your project and add Markdown Cursor rule files (.mdc
extension) for different scopes (global, or specific to certain files/folders). There are fantastic community resources with ready-made rules you can start from – for instance, the “awesome-cursorrules” repository has a curated list of .cursorrules files for various frameworks and languages. Browsing that list, you’ll even find entries for Python FastAPI best practices and possibly Streamlit. Indeed, there are multiple FastAPI-focused rules available (e.g. “Python (FastAPI Best Practices)” and “Python (FastAPI Scalable API)” in the collection). You could grab one of those as a base. Likewise, there might be a rule file for Streamlit or general Python GUI apps. Another great resource is the Cursor Rules repository by sparesparrow, which organizes many rules by category (framework, security, etc.) and explains how to structure them.
For our Daily Task Manager, let’s set two rule files as examples:
backend.fastapi.mdc
: Specifies guidelines for backend development using FastAPI. For example, “Use FastAPI’s dependency injection for database sessions,” “Use Pydantic models for request/response schemas,” “Follow RESTful API design for routes,” etc. This rule can ensure the AI scaffolds the API in an idiomatic FastAPI way (creating anapp.py
, including CORS middleware, usinguvicorn
for local runs, etc., depending on what you include in the rule).frontend.streamlit.mdc
: Specifies guidelines for Streamlit. For example, rules like “Usest.title
,st.button
,st.write
for UI components,” “Don’t use HTML directly, use Streamlit elements,” “Keep the app single-page with sections for today’s tasks and upcoming tasks,” etc. This will guide the AI to produce Streamlit code that fits our use case (maybe usingst.dataframe
orst.drag_and_drop
if available for implementing a Kanban board of tasks).
You can write these rules yourself or copy from the community. A typical rule file has a YAML header to scope when it applies and then a list of bullet-point instructions. Once you have them, add them to Cursor. You can do this by placing the .mdc
files in your repo’s .cursor/rules
folder (Cursor will auto-detect them), or by adding them as User Rules via the Cursor settings. In fact, if you want rules to apply to all your projects, you can save them as User Rules (go to Cursor Settings → Rules → User Rules and paste the content there). As one user describes, you can open the Command Palette (Cmd/Ctrl + Shift + P) and find “Rules: User Rules” to paste global rules. Project-specific rules, however, are best kept in the repo so they’re version-controlled and shared with your team.
With rules in place, when you start coding with Cursor’s agent, you’ll notice the AI adhering to them. It might automatically pick up on things like using your preferred database library or respecting your file structure conventions. Essentially, you’ve trained it on how to behave for this project. This upfront work pays off in more consistent, high-quality code generation. And if the AI ever seems to ignore a rule, you can remind it by referencing the rule file (e.g., “According to our backend.fastapi
rule, ensure all endpoints have proper exception handling.”).
(Side note: Cursor originally used a single .cursorrules
file (now legacy) for rules, but now the recommended approach is multiple .mdc
files. Both work, but the .mdc
approach is more powerful and organized.)
3. Choose the Right Agent Mode (AGENT vs ASK)
Cursor offers two primary modes when interacting with the AI in the chat interface: Agent mode and Ask mode. It’s crucial to understand the difference and choose the right one for each task.
- Agent Mode (often just called Composer or Auto mode): This is Cursor’s default, most powerful mode. In Agent mode, the AI acts autonomously to execute your request. It doesn’t just answer your question — it will plan a series of steps (possibly reading multiple files, writing new code, running commands) to fulfill your ask. Essentially, Agent mode can modify your codebase on its own. For example, if you say “Add a dark mode toggle to my app,” the Agent might locate relevant files, insert a theme state variable, update UI components, and even create a new CSS file, all in one go. It’s like having an AI developer who can read and write across your project autonomously. Agent mode has full tool access: it can search the codebase, open web docs, and even run terminal commands (with your approval). In short, use Agent mode when you want hands-off automation: implementing features, refactoring code, running tests, etc., with the AI doing the heavy lifting.
- Ask Mode: This is a more conservative, Q&A style mode. In Ask mode, the AI will not make changes to your files. It only reads and responds. Think of it as a super-smart Stack Overflow or a code consultant. Ask mode is great for when you want to explore or learn about the code without altering anything. For instance, you can use Ask mode to query, “What does the function
update_task_status
do?” or “Why am I getting a null pointer exception here?” The AI will search and read relevant files to answer you, but it won’t try to fix or write code. This read-only nature is perfect for planning and debugging – you can discuss solutions with the AI, get explanations, or have it sketch out code snippets, and then you decide what to do with that information. In our Daily Task Manager scenario, you might use Ask mode at the start to brainstorm the app’s architecture or to have the AI review the PRD and suggest a task breakdown, without committing any changes.
In practice, these modes map to how you “chat” with Cursor. Agent mode is akin to saying “Cursor, do this for me,” whereas Ask mode is “Cursor, tell me about this.” A good rule of thumb: Use Agent mode when you’re ready for action (code generation or edits), and Ask mode when you need understanding or a plan. For example, if you encounter a bug that you don’t understand, switch to Ask mode and ask Cursor to analyze the error — it will read your logs or code and explain the issue (but not fix it until you’re ready). Once you’re confident in the solution, you might switch to Agent mode and say “Apply the fix” or implement it yourself.
To illustrate: In our Task Manager app, suppose you want to implement the “Create Task” feature. You could start by using Ask mode to discuss the design: “How should I structure the FastAPI endpoint for creating a task and what database schema is appropriate?” The AI might reply with an outline of an endpoint and a suggestion to use an ORM model with fields X, Y, Z. Once you’re satisfied, you then switch to Agent mode and say, “Okay, implement the Create Task API as discussed.” In Agent mode, Cursor will generate the code across the necessary files to add this feature.
Remember, Agent mode = autonomy with safeguards, Ask mode = inquiry and planning. Both are extremely useful. Using them at the right times is key. (For completeness: Cursor also has a “Manual” mode for inline edits and a “Custom” mode, but those are beyond our scope. The main decision is between letting the agent drive or staying in Q&A mode.)
4. Select the Best Model for the Job
Behind Cursor’s AI features are various large language models (LLMs). Cursor lets you choose which model (or have it auto-select). Choosing an appropriate model can make a big difference in code quality and speed. Here are some guidelines:
- Use top-tier models for complex tasks or critical code generation. Models like Claude-4 Sonnet, OpenAI’s o3 and Google Gemini 2.5 Pro are considered state-of-the-art in mid-2025 for coding assistance. In evaluations, Claude 4 Sonnet and Gemini 2.5 Pro have shown best-in-class performance, with OpenAI’s “o3” model close behind. These models excel at understanding larger context and producing coherent, correct code. For our app, using one of these models in Agent mode would likely yield the best result when generating a complex feature (e.g., implementing drag-and-drop or an intricate filtering function).
- Be mindful of model context length. If your project is large or you need to feed a lot of information to the model (e.g. lengthy PRD, multiple files), you might need a model with an extended context window. “Max” models like Claude-4 Opus are designed for this: Opus can handle very long prompts (up to 200K tokens of context) and is tuned for deeper reasoning across large codebases. However, if your app is small/medium, you may not require that much context. In fact, Claude-4 Sonnet itself supports a 200K context window, which is quite large. Google’s Gemini 2.5 is reported to handle up to 1 million tokens— useful for truly massive projects or if you wanted to feed entire documentation manuals into context. The point is: match the model to your context needs. For our Task Manager (which is relatively small), using Sonnet or GPT-4.1 with ~100K or less context window is fine. You’d save Opus or Gemini’s huge context mainly for enterprise-scale projects or if you intend to load a lot of external documentation into Cursor.
- Consider speed and cost trade-offs. The most powerful models often have higher latency and cost. For example, Claude-4 Opus and OpenAI’s top models might be slower or consume more credits. Interestingly, one analysis found that Claude 4 Opus and OpenAI’s “o3” were more expensive but not significantly better in quality for many tasks, whereas Claude 4 Sonnet gave nearly the same performance at a fraction of the cost. In practice, this means you might reserve Opus (or other “Max” models) for when you truly need that capability (like analyzing huge logs or doing an hours-long agent session). For day-to-day coding tasks, the standard high-end model (Sonnet or GPT-4.1) could be more cost-effective and plenty capable. Cursor also often supports “fast” vs “slow” request pools — e.g., you might have a quota of fast responses on the best models, after which you either wait or pay extra. Plan accordingly: use the best model when quality matters (core logic, tricky problems) and consider using slightly smaller models for boilerplate or after you’ve run through quotas.
- Fallback to completion models when needed. If you find the fancy models are unavailable (perhaps due to rate limits or offline work), Cursor can use other completion models. For instance, GPT-4o (the original) or even GPT-4o-mini can be used for simpler code generation tasks. They won’t be as accurate with complex instructions, but for straightforward code (like generating a simple config file or a basic function), they might suffice. In Cursor’s model selector, “GPT-4.1” refers to OpenAI’s improved model (released Apr 2025) which generally outperforms the older GPT-4o. So if you must fall back, GPT-4.1 is preferred over GPT-4o. And if even those are not available or too slow, models like Claude 3.7 or Code Llama could be options — but expect to guide them more.
In summary, for our project: we’d likely use Claude-4 Sonnet or o3 in Agent mode for the heavy lifting (these models handle coding tasks extremely well and use our rules effectively). If we enable Max Mode for an extensive operation (say we want Cursor to ingest a whole design document or large dataset), we might switch to Claude-4 Opus temporarily. And we keep an eye on usage — if we hit limits, we can let Cursor fall back to slower/cheaper models or explicitly choose a lower-tier model for non-critical generations. Model selection is about balancing quality, context length, and cost/speed for each task. Fortunately, Cursor’s UI makes it easy to switch models, even mid-project, so you can experiment and find what works best.
5. Use @
References in Prompts
One of Cursor’s killer features is the ability to inject specific context directly into your AI prompts using @
references. This allows you to point the AI to exactly what it should consider, such as a file, a function, or some output text, rather than relying solely on the model’s memory or codebase indexing. When building our Daily Task Manager, effective use of @
references ensures the AI is looking at the right information, which leads to more accurate and relevant responses.
Here are the most useful @
commands and how to use them:
@File
/@Files
: Include the contents of a file in your prompt. For example, if you have a filemodels.py
that defines the Task schema, you can say “Refer to@models.py
and create a new API endpoint to create a Task.” By writing@models.py
in the chat, Cursor will attach that file’s content (or relevant chunks) to the prompt. This way, the AI has full context of yourTask
model while writing the new endpoint. Cursor even shows a preview when you type@...
so you can disambiguate files with similar names. If the file is large, Cursor will chunk it and retrieve only the parts likely relevant to your query. This is hugely helpful – it’s like telling the AI “here’s the exact spec to follow.” Always prefer@File
references over manually pasting code or hoping the AI remembers – it’s more reliable and doesn’t count toward token limits as heavily because of Cursor’s smart chunking.@Code
: Reference a specific snippet of code or a symbol from your codebase. Suppose you want to modify theupdate_task_status
function intasks.py
. You could copy-paste it into your prompt, but a cleaner way is: highlight that function in the editor and press Add to Chat (Ctrl+Shift+L) – this will insert an@Code
reference for that snippet. Or you can simply type@update_task_status
and Cursor’s code index will find and show that function (especially if it’s indexed or open). The@Code
symbol is great for pinpointing just the piece of code you’re talking about, rather than an entire file. For instance: “Optimize the following function for performance: @update_task_status” would give the AI only that function to focus on. This laser-focused context can make the AI’s job easier and its answer more specific.@Web
: Pull in information from the web. Cursor can actually perform web searches on-the-fly and include the results in context. For example, if you need to recall how Streamlit implements drag-and-drop, you could prompt: “Search Streamlit documentation for drag and drop. @Web ‘Streamlit drag and drop tasks’.” The@Web
command will make Cursor do a quick search; it will then scrape relevant content (perhaps from Streamlit’s docs or a forum) and feed it to the AI as reference. This is incredibly useful for up-to-date info or library documentation that isn’t in your codebase. In Agent mode, Cursor often does this automatically when needed (it will say something like “Searching the web for X”). But you can explicitly invoke it via@Web
to be sure. Another scenario: include a link directly – if you paste a URL, Cursor tags it as@Link
and will fetch the page or PDF content for you. For instance, “According to @Link(https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/tutorial/async-sql/), adjust our DB code to use async SQLAlchemy.” The AI will have that FastAPI doc content available to follow.@Terminal
: Include output from Cursor’s integrated terminal (or any command execution). When building your app, you’ll likely run it or run tests, and you might get errors or logs. Cursor captures terminal outputs, and with@terminal
you can insert those logs into the prompt. Imagine your test run showed an error stack trace forcreate_task
. You can say: “Fix the bug indicated by @terminal”, and the AI will see the entire error message and traceback. This helps it pinpoint the issue and suggest a fix. Essentially,@terminal
gives the AI eyes on your runtime behavior, which is invaluable for debugging. (Under the hood, Cursor 0.46+ supports terminal referencing so you can iterate faster on failing tests.) No more manually copying error texts; just call@terminal
and focus on the solution.@Git
and others: While not explicitly asked, it’s worth a quick mention that you can reference git history too.@git <commit_hash>
or just@git
in some contexts will show recent commits or diffs, which the AI can then explain or use to understand changes. This can be useful if you want to ask “what changed in the last commit?” or “why might the last commit have introduced a bug?”.
In practice, combining @
references makes your prompts grounded. For example: “Using @tasks.py and @models.py for context, implement the feature described in @instructions.md (the PRD).” This single prompt gives the AI everything: the PRD with requirements, the relevant code files for tasks and data models, etc., all at once. It then doesn’t have to guess or hallucinate names – it sees the real ones. The result is code that matches your existing structures and fulfills the spec more precisely.
To maximize this, exclude irrelevant context (we’ll cover indexing in tip #9). Keep your @
usage targeted. And remember, you can drag-and-drop files into the chat or use the right-click “Add to Chat” as shortcuts for making @
references. Once you get in the habit of using @
, you’ll wonder how you ever used AI coding assistants without it – it’s that helpful for grounding the AI in your actual codebase.
6. Write Detailed, Precise Prompts
When instructing Cursor’s AI, clarity is key. Large language models are powerful, but they’re not mind-readers — they do much better when you spell out exactly what you want. In coding, this means providing as much detail and context in your prompt as possible. A well-crafted prompt can be the difference between the AI delivering a correct, clean solution versus something off-base that requires heavy fixes.
Some best practices for prompt writing (illustrated with our Task Manager app):
- State the objective and constraints clearly. For example, instead of saying “Add a login feature”, you’d get better results with: “Add a user login feature to the app. Use JWT for authentication tokens and FastAPI’s OAuth2PasswordBearer for security. Include a
/login
API endpoint and update the Streamlit frontend with a login form. Only authenticated users should see the task board.” This prompt specifies what to do and how/with what tools. The AI now knows the exact scope and the libraries to use. Vague prompts often lead to the AI making assumptions (which could be wrong). Precise prompts guide it down the correct path. - Refer to specific components by name when possible. If your project already has certain modules, name them. E.g., “Modify the
Task
Pydantic model in @models.py to include a priority field (High, Medium, Low) and adjust any functions using it accordingly.” This is far better than “Add priority to tasks”, which leaves open questions (where to add? what type?). By pointing to@models.py
and even the class name, you remove ambiguity. One user rightly noted: don’t just say “fix it,” say “fix the error on line 20 ofuser.js
” – the more you pinpoint, the better the AI can help. - Provide examples if needed. For instance, “Format the output as:
Task 1 – [Done]
(ID, then title, then status in brackets) when printing tasks.” If you have a specific output format or input example, include it. The AI is good at following patterns – if you show one, it will mimic it. For our app, if you want the API response JSON to look a certain way, you could write that out in the prompt so the AI knows the expectation. - Specify the files to change or create. In a multi-file project, it helps to tell the agent where to put things. For example: “Create a new file
database.py
for database connection logic and updatemain.py
to initialize the DB usingdatabase.py
.” This way the AI won’t dump everything in one file or in the wrong place. When the AI knows the intended file structure, it follows it. In Cursor agent mode, you can even use directives like> Create file: database.py
within your prompt, but a simpler way is just describing the plan in English. In fact, an experienced Cursor user suggests: give Cursor a hint on how to build it, tell it which file and where to make changes. This leads to more organized outputs and fewer misplacements of code. - Mention any rules or context it should keep in mind. If you have important points from earlier in the conversation or from your rules file, reiterate them when it’s crucial. For example: “Recall our project rule: all functions must have logging. Ensure the new
create_task
function has an appropriate log message.” The AI will then certainly include it. While the model usually “remembers” prior discussion up to its context limit, being explicit never hurts. It acts as a reinforcement to the AI’s instructions.
The difference that detailed prompting makes is enormous. When you say “Implement X” generically, the AI might produce code that functionally works but ignores edge cases, lacks structure, or just doesn’t fit your style. By contrast, if you say exactly what you want (perhaps effectively writing a mini-spec in your prompt), the AI will produce something much closer to your vision on the first try. As one Medium author put it after using custom rules: “The more you tell the AI, the better it works. Don’t be afraid to be specific.”.
In our Daily Task Manager, a detailed prompt might look like:
“Implement a new feature: Task Search. In the Streamlit UI (
app.py
), add a search input box at the top. When the user types, filter the task list to only show tasks whose title or description contain the query (case-insensitive). Also, add a backend FastAPI endpoint/search?query=
that returns matching tasks as JSON. Use our existingTask
model and repository functions to perform the search (e.g., a SQL LIKE query on title/description). Ensure to update the frontend to call this API (perhaps usingrequests
in Streamlit) and display the results live as the user types. Include basic error handling (if query is too short, show a message). Follow the project’s logging rule: log each search query on the backend.**”
Yes, that prompt is long — but it’s clear. An AI reading that knows exactly what files and functions to produce or modify, what the acceptance criteria are, and even the user experience details. Writing such a prompt might take a minute, but it can save you hours of tweaking the output later. As a bonus, this approach forces you to think through the feature fully, which reduces miscommunication with the AI.
Finally, remember that prompt writing is an iterative skill. If the output you get is not what you envisioned, analyze your prompt. Did you omit something crucial? Did you leave any room for interpretation? Update your prompt and try again (we’ll discuss iterative improvements more in tip #8). Over time, you’ll develop an intuition for how the AI responds to phrasing and what level of detail yields the best results.
7. Request Logging, Unit Tests, and Documentation
A common mistake when using AI for coding is to focus only on the happy-path implementation. But robust software includes a lot more: logging for observability, unit tests for correctness, and documentation for maintainability. The good news is, Cursor can help with all of these — you just have to remember to ask! It might feel like extra work at first, but prompting the AI to handle tests or docs can significantly improve your project’s quality with minimal effort.
Logging: Make it a habit to have the AI include logging statements in the code it writes. Logs are your best friend when debugging issues in production or even during development. For example, if Cursor is generating a FastAPI endpoint, you can prompt: “Add logging: when a task is created, log an INFO message with the task ID.” The agent will then insert something like logger.info(f"Task {id} created")
in the appropriate place. Since our project rules emphasize robust observability, the AI should already lean towards including logs if well-guided. If it doesn’t, nudge it. For our Daily Task Manager, logs could include things like logging when a new task is added, when a task is moved between columns, when a user logs in, etc. This will pay off when you need to trace what happened in a user session. A quick prompt addition like “ensure to log each operation (for debugging)” is usually enough for Cursor to sprinkle logging throughout the code.
Unit Tests: It may sound surprising, but you can ask Cursor to write tests for you — and it’s quite good at it. In test-driven development fashion, you can even generate tests before implementing a feature to clarify the acceptance criteria. For example, “Write unit tests for the Task model and CRUD operations” could yield a test_tasks.py
module with test cases. A LinkedIn post about “vibe coding” suggests: “Ask Cursor to generate unit tests before new logic. Don’t move forward until they pass.” – meaning you can have the AI draft the tests, then implement until they pass. This structure-first approach can drastically reduce bugs. In our app, we might prompt Cursor: “Generate pytest unit tests for the task creation API (covering success, missing fields, invalid data)”. The AI will produce test functions using e.g. client.post("/tasks", ...)
and asserting the results. These tests not only validate the code it just wrote but also serve as documentation of expected behavior. Whenever you implement a non-trivial piece of logic with Cursor, follow up with “Now write tests for it”. It’s much easier for the AI to do it when the context is fresh. The result is a suite of tests you can run to immediately verify the code – giving you confidence that the AI’s code actually works. It’s a good practice to include tests as part of the definition of “done” for a feature. The AI, guided by your instructions, can create comprehensive tests (including edge cases you might forget). For instance, it might include a test for “creating a task with an empty title should return 422” if your FastAPI model has a constraint – things like that. And if any test fails, you know exactly where to iterate.
Documentation: Don’t leave documentation as an afterthought. Cursor can generate documentation content, from docstrings in your functions to README sections. If you want every function to have a docstring, mention that in your rules or prompts (Cursor will oblige by describing the function’s purpose and parameters). For higher-level docs, like a README.md for your project, you can actually prompt Cursor to write it! e.g., “Create a README.md summarizing how to install and run the Daily Task Manager app, and a short user guide.” This can yield a decent first draft of documentation that you can then polish. Similarly, you might ask for usage examples or inline comments if you feel the code needs explanation. The AI might not spontaneously add a lot of comments (often its code is self-explanatory), but if you ask, it will. There’s also the aspect of updating design docs: if your PRD changed, you could ask Cursor to update the PRD or changelog accordingly. The bottom line is, treat documentation as a first-class citizen in your prompts. One pro tip from the community: use the AI beyond just writing code — let it handle docs, configurations, even tasks like writing an API spec. These “side outputs” can save you time. In our project, after implementing core features, we might have Cursor generate a “User Guide” Markdown that explains how to use the app (with steps like creating tasks, moving them, filtering, etc.). This not only is handy if others will use your app, but it also forces you (and the AI) to think from the user’s perspective, potentially revealing missing pieces.
Why is it worth prompting for these things? Because skipping them might save a few minutes now, but will cost hours later. Logs help diagnose issues in production. Tests prevent regressions and give confidence to refactor or extend features. Documentation helps onboard others (or reminds you, a month from now, what the system’s supposed to do!). By having Cursor do 90% of the work on these, there’s little excuse to omit them. One of Cursor’s philosophies is to be an “AI teammate” — so let your teammate handle the tedious but important stuff, not just the shiny feature code. Many great Cursor rule sets explicitly enforce testing and observability as primary concerns, emphasizing that idiomatic, maintainable code goes hand-in-hand with these practices.
So, next time you finish a feature with Cursor, consider your definition of done: Code implemented? Okay. But also: Tests written? Logging in place? Docs updated? If not, spend a few extra prompts to have Cursor do those. For instance: “Write a docstring for each of these new functions explaining their behavior and parameters.”, “Generate a section in README under ‘Features’ describing the new search functionality.”, or “Add a log statement at WARN level if a search query is too short (less than 3 chars).” These are quick asks that result in a much more professional and robust project. As developers often say, if it’s worth doing, it’s worth automating — and Cursor is there to automate those best practices for you.
8. Improve Prompts Iteratively
Using Cursor is not a one-and-done interaction. Much like coding itself, it’s an iterative process. Rarely will your first prompt yield a perfect solution (especially for complex tasks). Instead of feeling disappointed or trying to manually fix everything, embrace an approach of prompt, review, refine, repeat. Each iteration teaches both you and the AI something, leading to progressively better results.
Here’s how to iterate effectively:
- Review the AI’s output thoroughly. When Cursor’s agent produces code, take a step back and read it as if you were reviewing a teammate’s pull request. Does it meet the requirements from the PRD? Is the logic sound? Did it follow your rules (e.g., are logs and tests there if you asked for them)? Often you’ll spot something that’s not quite right or could be improved. For example, maybe the function works but isn’t using the most efficient approach, or the UI looks a bit off in wording or layout. Make a note of these.
- Identify why any issues happened. This is key. If the AI made a wrong assumption or missed a detail, it’s usually because of the way the prompt was phrased or the context given. Perhaps your prompt was ambiguous or incomplete. For instance, if it named a variable differently than your codebase convention, maybe you didn’t specify the naming convention. Or if it didn’t handle a certain edge case, maybe you didn’t mention that scenario. By pinpointing the cause, you can address it in the next prompt.
- Refine your prompt with the new insights. Now that you know what needs changing, formulate a new instruction. This can be a follow-up prompt (continuing the chat) or, if things went really astray, you might start a fresh chat with a revised prompt that incorporates the fixes from scratch. In many cases, iterating in the same session is fine: e.g., “The solution looks good, but please update it to use a binary search for efficiency” or “It’s missing the priority label feature — add that following the same pattern”. Cursor will then modify the code accordingly. In other cases, you might realize the prompt should have been structured differently. Some developers literally regenerate entire components multiple times, each time tweaking the prompt or rules to see different outcomes and pick the best. This is actually a great way to use Cursor as a creative partner — e.g., “Generate the task board UI using a table.”, then “Now try using three columns with Drag and Drop.”, compare the two implementations, and choose the better.
- Leverage Cursor’s memory and suggestions. Cursor’s agent is built to handle iterative development. It will often remember your last corrections. For example, if you correct a bug and then ask for another feature, it might proactively avoid similar bugs. Also, Cursor creates checkpoints before big changes, so you can always restore if an iteration goes wrong. Don’t hesitate to roll back and try a different approach if needed. As the White Prompt blog demonstrated, AI-assisted development becomes a cycle: AI generates code, we review and refine it, then repeat — each loop improving the application. Keep that mindset.
- Regenerate the whole app (in parts) to learn patterns. One interesting strategy is to have Cursor build the app (or major components) more than once. For instance, you could prompt one way initially. Later, after learning more (perhaps after manually tweaking things), you could write a new prompt that encapsulates all those lessons and let it generate again (maybe in a new branch or scratch project). You might discover that the second time, Cursor produces an even cleaner structure. This is not wasted effort — it’s like a second draft in writing. Sometimes merging the best of both drafts yields a superior final product. Additionally, by regenerating, you can compare how consistent the agent is or see if it introduces new ideas you hadn’t thought of. Over multiple generations, you’ll notice Cursor’s patterns: maybe it always sets up certain files or uses particular libraries. If you like those patterns, great. If not, adjust your rules to change them.
- Don’t be afraid to intervene manually if needed, then inform Cursor. Iteration doesn’t mean the AI must do 100% of the edits. If it’s a small thing, you might just edit the code yourself (e.g., rename a variable for clarity or refactor a loop). After that, you can tell Cursor what you did: “I renamed
x
totask_id
for clarity. Continue with that naming going forward.” This updates the AI’s context and prevents it from reintroducing the old name. You are still the lead developer; think of Cursor as a junior dev who works super fast but might need guidance. Iteration is your code review and mentorship process for this AI “dev”.
The key philosophy here is continuous improvement. Each AI generation is not the final answer — it’s a draft. Use it, test it, poke holes in it, and then make the next draft better. This echoes how software development normally works (write, test, debug, refactor) but at an accelerated pace. With AI, you can turn around an iteration in seconds or minutes instead of hours. Exploit that speed — try multiple approaches, refine wording, and see how it impacts the result. You will quickly develop an intuition for prompt engineering and for Cursor’s quirks and strengths.
In summary: iterate, iterate, iterate. Use each Cursor output as feedback to refine your instructions. Over multiple cycles, you’ll converge on a solution that meets your needs. And don’t get discouraged by a less-than-perfect first attempt — that’s normal. As the saying goes, “If at first you don’t succeed, prompt, prompt again.” With practice, you might even enjoy the process of steering the AI and watching the code evolve through your guidance.
9. Exclude Unnecessary Files from Indexing
Imagine you have a large repository with thousands of files, or even just a bunch of directories like node_modules
, logs, or archived data. You probably don’t want or need the AI to consider all that when working on your app. That’s where Cursor’s indexing ignore features come into play. By excluding irrelevant or huge files from Cursor’s index, you streamline the context and improve performance (both in terms of speed and relevant suggestions). It’s akin to telling the AI, “Focus on this, ignore that.” This will make Cursor faster and more accurate.
Cursor automatically respects your .gitignore
– any files ignored by Git are not indexed for AI context by default. But you often have additional needs beyond that. For instance, maybe you do have documentation or specs in the repo that you want to keep (not git-ignored), but you don’t want them clogging up the AI’s brain unless explicitly referenced. Or perhaps you have a huge data/
folder with sample JSON or CSV files that aren’t code.
Use .cursorignore
and .cursorindexignore
to manage this. Here’s the difference:
.cursorignore
: Files listed here are completely invisible to Cursor – it won’t index them and you cannot reference them with@
either. Use this for truly irrelevant stuff. For example, compiled bytecode, large binaries, dependency folders, etc. If our Task Manager app had, say, avenv/
or a front-end build directory, those can go in.cursorignore
. They are of no use to the AI..cursorindexignore
: Files here are not indexed automatically, but you can still reference them explicitly. This is handy for things you generally don’t want AI to waste cycles on unless needed. The tip from an experienced user: “If you have adocs/
folder with a lot of markdown files that you might occasionally reference, put it in .cursorindexignore. Cursor won’t index all those files by default, but you can still do@docs/featureX.md
when you need it.”. This keeps the default context clean, but you have on-demand access. In our case, if we had a large design document or an old PRD we don’t want constantly loaded, we could cursor-index-ignore it. The AI won’t be distracted by it unless we explicitly bring it in via an@Link
or similar.
By trimming the index, you help Cursor focus on what matters. It also prevents hitting token limits with irrelevant data. And practically, it can reduce the latency of suggestions since there’s less to search.
How to do it? Simply create those files (.cursorignore
and/or .cursorindexignore
) at your project root, similar to .gitignore
syntax (patterns of file/folder names). For example, your .cursorignore
might contain:
venv/
node_modules/
*.log
.env
And .cursorindexignore
might contain:
docs/
design_specs/
legacy_code/
This means “don’t index these, but keep them referenceable.” Now, within Cursor, you can verify what’s indexed via the Context -> Codebase Indexing view, and you can always adjust these files as needed.
For our Daily Task Manager, maybe we decide that the README.md
and CHANGELOG.md
should be indexed (so the AI knows what’s in them), but a large LICENSE
file or some data/sample_tasks.json
file should not. We’d put the latter in .cursorignore
if it’s not useful at all, or .cursorindexignore
if it’s only occasionally needed. By doing this, when we ask Cursor something general like “Analyze the project for any obvious issues,” it won’t waste time going through license text or example data – it’ll stick to real code and relevant docs.
The result is a more efficient AI assistant. Think of it as decluttering your AI’s mental workspace. Not to mention, if there are huge files (like a 10k-line JSON), excluding them avoids running into context size limits inadvertently.
One more benefit: security and privacy. If you have secrets or credentials in some files (hopefully not committed to git, but just in case), adding them to .cursorignore
ensures Cursor won’t read them. Always a good practice.
In short, be deliberate about what Cursor “sees.” Just as you wouldn’t feed extraneous info to a human collaborator when it’s not needed, don’t let the AI get distracted by irrelevant files. As the pro tip goes: use .cursorignore
for stuff the AI should never see, and .cursorindexignore
for stuff it need not automatically see but can access on demand. This tuning can make Cursor’s responses more focused and its operations snappier.
After configuring these, you might notice that Chat responses improve in relevance, since they’re drawing from a tighter knowledge base. And when you do need something from the ignored set, you have the @
tool to explicitly bring it in – best of both worlds.
10. Utilize MCP Servers (Advanced)
Finally, let’s touch on MCP servers — a fairly advanced topic, but worth knowing exists as you grow with Cursor. MCP stands for Model Context Protocol (in Cursor’s terminology). Essentially, it allows Cursor to fetch context from external sources or specialized knowledge bases in a smart way, beyond just your local files and the web search. Think of MCP servers as plugins or extensions that feed the AI with domain-specific info or perform heavy-duty context processing.
For example, the community has built MCP servers like Context7 and DeepWiki. These can be configured to provide, say, a vector search over a large documentation set, or to maintain a long conversation memory beyond normal limits. There are also official MCP endpoints for popular frameworks — the tip mentions a Nuxt.js MCP (mcp.nuxt.com
) which likely serves Nuxt-specific guidance to the AI. In practice, using an MCP might mean the AI can retrieve very specific framework internals or patterns that would be too much to store in rules or too dynamic for static docs.
How might this help with our project? If, hypothetically, we had a huge knowledge base or we wanted the AI to be aware of something like “common task management app designs” or a company’s internal guidelines, we could set up an MCP server that contains that info and let Cursor query it when needed. It’s like giving Cursor a superpower: the ability to pull in expansive context on demand. Some MCP servers even allow running custom code or tools as part of the AI’s thought process (like a REPL, etc., which you might see in future Cursor features).
However, as the instruction suggests, MCPs are an advanced option and could fill an entire article on their own. Setting them up often requires running a separate service or using community templates, and understanding how to integrate it in Cursor’s settings (usually a JSON config pointing to the MCP server). The average user building a simple to-do app probably won’t need this. But it’s good to know it’s there as you scale up.
Consider MCP when you notice the AI hitting context limits or lacking knowledge that is available somewhere else. For instance, if your project grows into a suite of microservices with tons of docs, an MCP could aggregate those docs so Cursor can always answer questions about any service. Or if you’re working with something cutting-edge like a new library that the base model wasn’t trained on, you could have an MCP that interfaces with that library’s docs or source code to help the AI. It’s somewhat analogous to how some coding assistants connect to documentation databases — MCP is a general mechanism for that.
For now, if you’re new to Cursor, just be aware: there’s a feature called “Max Mode” (which we touched on with large contexts) and MCP that can supercharge the agent’s capabilities. These typically come into play for large, complex projects or when building your own AI-driven tools on top of Cursor. The Cursor forum and docs have more examples of using custom MCP servers for things like retrieving context from StackOverflow or handling very long running tasks.
To use an MCP server in Cursor, you’d configure it in the .cursor/mcp.json
file. Tip #5 from the Instructa blog suggests doing this to share config with a team. For example, you might add an entry for an MCP’s URL and an API key if needed. Once set up, Cursor’s agent will know it has extra tools. You might see it in action when the agent’s reasoning says something like “Using MCP server to retrieve X…”.
Since our task is a simple Daily Task Manager, we likely don’t need MCP. But it’s worth mentioning in this best practices list because for performance boosters, especially in bigger contexts, MCPs can be game-changers. They effectively break the normal context window barrier by providing a form of extended memory or specialized skills.
Important: Using MCP or enabling Max mode can incur additional costs (since they often rely on large context models or external API calls). So, use them intentionally, when the benefit outweighs the cost.
In summary, MCP servers are like bonus power-ups for Cursor that allow deeper integration with external knowledge and tools. They are overkill for small apps, but keep them in mind as you push Cursor to its limits. It’s one more way to customize your AI development environment to your specific needs.
(For those interested, the Cursor community forum and documentation have more on setting up MCP servers. This goes beyond best practices and into advanced territory, so we won’t dive deeper here.)
Conclusion
Building a Daily Task Manager app with Cursor IDE has hopefully illustrated how AI can transform the development process — if used strategically. Cursor is far more than “VS Code with ChatGPT slapped on”; it’s an AI pair-programmer that, when properly guided, can handle much of the heavy lifting of coding. We started by planning the project with a PRD, set clear rules so the AI follows our tech stack conventions, and then iteratively built out the app by choosing the right modes and models for each task. We leaned on @
references to give the AI precise context, wrote detailed prompts to eliminate ambiguity, and even had Cursor generate tests, logs, and docs to round out the project. Along the way, we kept things efficient by focusing the AI on what matters (ignoring noise via ignore files) and noted the potential of advanced features like MCP for future enhancements.
The overarching theme is intentionality. Using Cursor effectively means being intentional about how you engage with the AI. If you just treat it as a fancy autocomplete and let it code willy-nilly, you might end up frustrated or with subpar code. That’s the “naive” usage that can be counterproductive — for example, blindly accepting every suggestion without reviewing, or not giving it clear direction, could lead to a mess. But when you apply the best practices we discussed, you turn Cursor into a truly powerful ally. It’s the difference between having an intern who’s unsupervised versus one who has a clear task list and guidance — the latter will surprise you with how much they can accomplish.
In practice, developers who invest time in writing a good initial prompt or setting up rules often report that Cursor “feels like it reads their mind” — because they gave it their mind, in the form of well-structured instructions. By contrast, those who skip these steps might feel Cursor is hit-or-miss. Now you know why: the value you get from Cursor is proportional to the clarity of guidance you give it.
As you continue using Cursor, you’ll likely develop your own workflow tweaks and additional rules that suit your style. The 10 best practices here are a foundation. They ensure that you cover the important bases (planning, context, quality, iteration, etc.). With these in place, you can tackle bigger and more complex projects confidently. And frankly, it’s quite fun to collaborate with an AI agent when it’s operating at this level — it’s like pair programming with a colleague who writes code at 2am while you sleep, and you wake up to features implemented (with tests!).
Remember: Cursor’s real power shines when you combine human insight with AI speed. You define what needs to be done and how (at a high level), and let Cursor worry about the grunt work of actually writing the code, following the rules, checking the docs, etc. This synergy can make you an order of magnitude more productive. It transforms development into a higher-level activity — more thinking and directing, less typing and Googling.
So, as you build your next app (be it a task manager or anything else), put these best practices to use. Generate that PRD. Set up your project rules. Communicate clearly with the AI. Iterate towards excellence. Exclude the noise. And explore the advanced possibilities as you grow. Using Cursor wisely not only makes development faster, it also pushes you to be a better planner and communicator — skills that benefit any software project.
In the end, you’ll find that you’re delivering features faster and with more consistency than ever before. And perhaps the best part: you can focus on the creative and challenging aspects of software engineering, while Cursor handles the boilerplate and repetitive stuff in the background. That’s the promise of an AI-powered IDE — and with the strategies outlined here, you can fully realize that promise. Happy coding, and happy prompting!
